text: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments
textPreview: AI Playbook and Toolkit for Public Health Departments

alt: AI Playbook for Public Health logo

text:
Welcome participants to the Community Engagement for AI lesson.
Explain that this deck is designed for staff training, leadership orientation, governance preparation, or cross-program discussion.
Frame the topic in plain language: Community engagement helps departments understand how AI-supported workflows may affect trust, access, surveillance concerns, language needs, disability access, and public accountability.
Invite participants to think about where this topic already appears in their department's workflows, vendor tools, data systems, communications, or planning conversations.
Community engagement helps departments understand how AI-supported workflows may affect trust, access, surveillance concerns, language needs, disability access, and public accountability.
textPreview: Community engagement helps departments understand how AI-supported workflows may affect trust, access, surveillance concerns, language ne...

text: Engagement helps departments avoid AI uses that appear efficient internally but create confusion, mistrust, stigma, surveillance concerns, or access barriers externally.
textPreview: Engagement helps departments avoid AI uses that appear efficient internally but create confusion, mistrust, stigma, surveillance concerns...

text: Public-facing or community-impacting AI can erode trust if affected groups are not engaged before decisions are made.
textPreview: Public-facing or community-impacting AI can erode trust if affected groups are not engaged before decisions are made.

text:
Use this slide in the Community Engagement for AI training module.
Use this slide to explain the practical public health reason for learning this topic before AI tools are approved or deployed.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 19, 20, 21, 22, 46.
Emphasize the core guardrail: Public-facing or community-impacting AI can erode trust if affected groups are not engaged before decisions are made.
Ask participants how this topic could affect their own program area, staff roles, community relationships, data, or public communications.
By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:
textPreview: By the end of this lesson, staff should be able to:

text:
• Explain Community Engagement for AI in plain language.
• Identify appropriate public health uses and limits.
• Describe human review, governance, equity, privacy, and monitoring expectations.
• Connect the topic to the related plays and tools.

text:
Use this slide in the Community Engagement for AI training module.
Walk through the objectives and connect each one to a practical implementation decision.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 19, 20, 21, 22, 46.
Explain that participants do not need technical expertise to use this material. The goal is enough shared understanding to participate in governance, readiness assessment, use case review, procurement, deployment, or monitoring.

Community engagement for AI should happen before use cases are finalized, especially when a system affects public communication, surveillance, outreach, service access, prioritization, or resource allocation. Engagement helps departments surface concerns that technical testing may miss.

What the AI-supported workflow will and will not do. What data are involved and how privacy is protected. What decisions remain human and how concerns can be raised. How language, disability, digital access, rural context, Tribal context,.

text: Listening sessions, advisory groups, community partner consultation, usability testing, plain-language review, and feedback forms. Internal engagement with frontline staff who understand how communities experience department services. Ongoing.
textPreview: Listening sessions, advisory groups, community partner consultation, usability testing, plain-language review, and feedback forms. Intern...

text: Document who was engaged, what concerns were raised, what changed, and what remains unresolved. Route unresolved community concerns to governance. Close the feedback loop with plain-language summaries.

Use this slide in the Community Engagement for AI training module.
Use this slide to turn the concept into practical behaviors, decisions, and safeguards.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 19, 20, 21, 22, 46.
Invite participants to identify where these practices would need to be documented in their own department.
Start with Engagement Focus Areas, then compare it with the other practice areas on the slide.
Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.
textPreview: Use the topic to examine real workflows, not abstract technology.

text:
Use this slide in the Community Engagement for AI training module.
Use this slide to make the topic concrete through public health workflow examples.
Connect the discussion back to the playbook: this topic supports Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 19, 20, 21, 22, 46.
For each example, ask: What problem is being solved? What data would be used? Who reviews the AI output? What decision remains human? What could go wrong?

Which communities or partners may be affected by the proposed AI use? What should be explained in plain language before implementation? How will community feedback change governance, design, monitoring, or communication?

Use this slide for a facilitated activity near the end of the lesson.

Give participants time to work individually or in small groups. Ask them to produce a practical artifact that can feed into readiness assessment, governance review, use case prioritization, or implementation planning.
Expected takeaways include: AI community engagement plan, Plain-language AI explanation, Feedback-to-decision log.
Use this topic with Plays 1, 4, 7, 10, 12, 13 and Tools 1, 3, 19, 20, 21, 22, 46. Turn the discussion into a documented question, decision, gap, or implementation artifact.

Close by connecting the lesson to next actions and resources.
